key_insight: The paper builds a hybrid statistical-and-physical pipeline to generate synthetic 3D fiber microstructures that closely match real X-ray-μCT misalignment behavior in composite materials.
engineering_relevance: It enables simulation-ready, statistically equivalent composite geometries for virtual testing, uncertainty studies, and design of as-manufactured fiber-reinforced structures.
method: Extract per-slice and per-fiber in-plane/out-of-plane misalignment using an ellipse-intersection analysis of μCT slices.
Fit a stochastic model with slice-wise distributions, copula-based dependence, latent autoregression, and rare extreme-misalignment motifs.
Generate non-overlapping 3D fibers via variable-radius seeding, slice-by-slice growth, Delaunay neighborhoods, and ellipse-based contact resolution.
evidence_signal: The optimized model matched the original statistical descriptors closely, with deviations generally below 10%, and generated about 2400 synthetic fibers.
watch_item: The approach depends on careful hyperparameter calibration and geometric contact handling, which may be computationally demanding and sensitive to dataset-specific misalignment statistics.
paper_title: Autoregressive Modelling and Synthetic Generation of High-Fidelity, Statistically Equivalent 3D Microstructures for As-Manufactured Misalignments in Fiber-Reinforced Composites
